author: Robert Carr
description: A toasted ciabatta sandwich with warmed salami or chorizo, melted cheddar or mozzarella, quick pickle-brine onion, crisp pickles, greens, and Dijon mayo.

groceryIngredients.item: ciabatta roll or sturdy hoagie roll

ingredients: 1 small ciabatta roll or 2 slices ciabatta bread, split
1 1/2 ounces salami or chorizo, thinly sliced
1 1/2 ounces cheddar or low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella, sliced thin
A few very thin slices yellow onion
3 to 4 dill pickle slices, patted dry
Small handful baby arugula or lettuce, patted dry
1 teaspoon pickle brine from the jar
instructions: Stir the mayonnaise, Dijon mustard, and 1/2 teaspoon pickle brine together in a small bowl.
Toss the onion slices with the remaining 1/2 teaspoon pickle brine and let them sit while the skillet heats.
Warm a skillet over medium-low heat for 1 minute.
Add the salami or chorizo in a single loose layer and warm for 45 to 60 seconds per side, just until glossy and lightly curled at the edges. Transfer to a plate.
Add the butter to the skillet, then place the ciabatta cut-side down in the melted butter and cured-meat fat.
Toast for 1 to 2 minutes, until the cut sides are crisp and lightly browned.
Spread most of the Dijon mayo on the cut sides of the toasted ciabatta.
Layer the cheese on the bottom bread, then add the warmed salami or chorizo and the drained onion slices.
Close the sandwich and cook it in the skillet for 2 to 3 minutes per side, pressing lightly with a spatula, until the bread is crisp and the cheese softens.
Open the sandwich carefully, add the pickles, arugula or lettuce, and the remaining Dijon mayo, then close it again.
Cut in half and eat while the bread is crisp, the meat is warm, and the greens are still cool.
notes: Black truffle mustard seed salami pairs well with mozzarella; chorizo pairs well with cheddar.
Warming the salami or chorizo first deepens the cured meat flavor, and butter helps the ciabatta brown.
Medium-low heat gives the cheese time to soften before the ciabatta gets too dark.
If the ciabatta is crisp before the cheese softens, lower the heat and cover the skillet for 30 to 60 seconds.
Add the pickles and greens after toasting so they stay crisp instead of steaming.
Chorizo makes the sandwich smokier and spicier. Mild salami is gentler. Prosciutto works too, but avoid crisping it hard because it can turn leathery.
If the ciabatta feels too thick, pinch out a little bread from the inside before building the sandwich.
title: Ciabatta Salami Melt
